accept: act dep add prints a confirmation that unambiguously names which side is blocked. Suggested: 'act-A is now blocked by act-B' (or 'act-A relates to act-B' for relates type).
act show emits the dep line as 'blocked-by: <id>' or 'dep: blocked-by <id>' for blocks-type, not 'dep: blocks <id>'. Other types render with their natural directional name.
If both directions of a relationship need to be visible (incoming + outgoing), they appear under separate labels — e.g. 'blocks: <id>' for issues this one blocks, 'blocked-by: <id>' for the inverse.
JSON shape on act show changes consistently if needed — schema bump documented in spec.
payload.description:
Discovered while bootstrapping act on its own backlog (2026-05-10). Two display strings invert the natural-English reading of dep direction:

1. act dep add A B --type blocks (where A is blocked by B) prints: 'Edge A --[blocks]--> B already present'. Reads as 'A blocks B', means the opposite.
2. act show A on a blocked issue prints: 'dep: blocks <blocker-id>'. Reads as 'A blocks <blocker-id>', means 'A is blocked by <blocker-id>'.

Took me a full minute and an act show + ready cross-check to convince myself the deps were correct. An agent without this option is going to file deps wrong half the time.

Related to act-63a1 (directional aliases on the input side) but distinct — that issue covers --blocks / --blocked-by flags on dep add input. This one is about output display strings on the *show* and *confirmation* paths, which the alias issue does not touch.
payload.title: dep direction in display strings reads as inverse of actual meaning
